mission.title: Install and Configure Nats on Kubernetes
mission.description: NATS is a high-performance messaging system designed for cloud and edge native applications. Installing NATS allows you to build distributed systems that are simple, secure, and efficient, making it an essential tool for modern application architectures.

steps.title: Add the NATS Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the NATS Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration to access the latest charts.
```bash
helm repo add nats https://nats-io.github.io/k8s/helm/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install NATS using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install NATS with the specified version in a new namespace called 'nats'.
```bash
helm install nats nats/nats --version 2.12.4 --namespace nats --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the NATS installation
steps.description:
Check that the NATS pods are running successfully in the 'nats' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace nats
```

steps.title: Port-forward to access NATS
steps.description:
To access the NATS server locally, set up port forwarding:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/nats --namespace nats 4222:4222
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will show the NATS pods running in the 'nats' namespace and allow you to access the NATS server via port 4222 on your local machine. You can further configure resource limits to optimize your deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add nats https://nats-io.github.io/k8s/helm/charts
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the NATS installation, uninstall the Helm release using the following command. This will delete all associated Kubernetes resources created by the release.
```bash
helm uninstall nats --namespace nats
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
If there are any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) or persistent volume claims (PVCs) created by NATS, clean them up using the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd natsclusters.nats.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace nats
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the 'nats' namespace to remove any remaining resources and verify that it has been deleted:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace nats
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's crucial to back up the current state of your NATS deployment. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace nats -o yaml > nats-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the NATS release to the desired version. Replace '2.12.5' with the version you wish to upgrade to:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade nats nats/nats --version 2.12.5 --namespace nats
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check that the NATS pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace nats
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If your NATS pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the pod logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace nats
```
Common issues may include misconfigured resource limits or insufficient permissions.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the NATS service, ensure that the service is running and the correct ports are exposed:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace nats
```
If the service is not running, check the deployment status for errors.

troubleshooting.title: High memory usage
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice high memory usage, review the resource limits set for the NATS deployment. You can check the current resource usage with:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace nats
```
Consider adjusting the resource limits in the deployment.

troubleshooting.title: Unable to connect to NATS
troubleshooting.description:
If you are unable to connect to the NATS server, verify the port-forwarding command is running correctly:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/nats --namespace nats 4222:4222
```
Ensure there are no firewall rules blocking the connection.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
